1: Boaz goes to 'the gate' (haššaʿar) — the city gate was the formal legal venue in ancient Israel, equivalent to a courthouse: 'he sat down there' (wayyēšeḇ šām) in the position of a presiding authority. The nearer redeemer is summoned with the circumlocution pəlōnî ʾalmōnî — literally 'so-and-so' (no name given) — a deliberate anonymizing by the narrator: the man who refuses to perpetuate the dead man's name has his own name suppressed in the record. The scene has all the formal features of Israelite covenant-making at the gate: ten elders as witnesses, a formal recitation of terms, public declaration of acceptance or refusal, and the symbolic gesture ratifying the transaction.
5: Boaz's escalation of the terms is the legal pivot: 'On the day you buy the field from the hand of Naomi, you also acquire Ruth the Moabite, the widow of the dead, in order to restore the name of the dead to his inheritance' (lᵉhāqîm šēm hammēt ʿal naḥǎlātô). The phrase lᵉhāqîm šēm — to raise up a name — is the levirate obligation of Deut 25:5-10: when a man dies without sons, his brother is to marry the widow 'to perpetuate his name in Israel' (wəqāmāh šəm ʾāḥîw bəyiśrāʾēl). The name (šēm) in Hebrew thought is the continuation of personhood and family identity — 'cutting off the name' (kārat šēm) is equivalent to annihilating the person from Israel's memory and inheritance. What Boaz undertakes is therefore not merely a legal transaction but a raising of the dead from oblivion.
6: The nearer redeemer's refusal — lōʾ ʾûḵal liḡʾol lî pᵉn-ʾašḥît ʾet naḥǎlātî — 'I cannot redeem for myself, lest I ruin my own inheritance' — is legal rather than moral: taking Ruth and producing children in Mahlon's name would divide his estate between his own heirs and the adopted line. The verb šāḥat (ruin/destroy) is strong. He is calculating that the cost of redemption exceeds what he is willing to pay. This is precisely what makes Boaz's willingness significant: Boaz accepts the cost that the nearer redeemer refuses. The typological weight of the scene is carried by this contrast: there was one who had the right but would not pay; there was one who both had the right and willingly bore the cost.
7: The sandal ceremony (šālaph naʿălô, drawing off the sandal) ratifies the legal transfer by symbolic gesture: 'and this was the custom formerly in Israel concerning redeeming and exchanging: to confirm a transaction, the one drew off his sandal and gave it to the other.' The Deut 25:9-10 levirate ceremony involves the spurned widow pulling off the refusing brother's sandal in disgrace; here the nearer redeemer draws off his own sandal to cede the right voluntarily — a related but distinct gesture. The sandal (naʿal) represents the right of walking on the land: transferring it transfers the right to the property and all its obligations. The narrator's parenthetical explanation suggests this custom had become archaic by the time of writing — the book is recording not just a story but a legal-cultural world.
10: Boaz's formal declaration before the witnesses climaxes in the šēm clause: ûšəm hammēt lōʾ yikkārēt mēʿim ʾeḥāyw ûmišʿar məqômô — 'so that the name of the dead may not be cut off from among his brothers and from the gate of his place.' The phrase yikkārēt šēm (the name being cut off) is the OT's starkest formulation of personal extinction — to have one's name cut off is to cease to exist in communal memory, to have no inheritance, no descendants, no ongoing participation in Israel's story. Boaz's legal act is a rescue from annihilation: he raises up a name that was about to be cut off. The verb kārat (cut off) is the same used for the death penalty and for covenant-making (covenant = kārat berît, cutting a covenant); to prevent the name from being cut off is to preserve covenant continuity.
17: 'The women of the neighborhood gave him a name, saying, “A son has been born to Naomi”' — yullad bēn lᵉnoʿomî. The child Obed is formally born to Naomi by adoption-language, completing the lᵉhāqîm šēm purpose of the redemption: Elimelech's family line is not cut off but continues through Obed. The name ʿôḇēḏ means 'servant/worshipper' (ʿāḇaḏ, to serve/worship). The genealogy that follows (vv18-22) — from Perez (the son of Judah by Tamar, another story of unexpected covenant inclusion) through to David — is the book's final statement: the story of a Moabite woman's hesed and a kinsman-redeemer's willing sacrifice is the genealogical foundation of the Davidic line.
18: The closing genealogy (vv18-22) connects the Ruth narrative to the Davidic covenant: Perez → Hezron → Ram → Amminadab → Nahshon → Salmon → Boaz → Obed → Jesse → David. This is the Judahite line that Matt 1:3-6 reproduces verbatim in the genealogy of Jesus. The book of Ruth is not merely a story of covenant loyalty but the genealogical foundation document of the Davidic monarchy — and through it, the messianic line. The Gentile woman (Ruth the Moabite, Matt 1:5) who chose YHWH and enacted hesed is in the direct biological line of the Son of God. The book answers the question: where does David come from? The answer is: from a Moabite woman's covenant loyalty and a kinsman-redeemer's willing sacrifice — both of which are types of what the final Son of David will himself be and do.
2: vayyiqqach asarah anashim miziqnei ha-ir vayyomer shevu-poh vayeshvu: 'Then Boaz took ten of the elders of the town and said: “Sit down here.” They sat down.' The ten elders constitute a legal quorum for the formal proceedings. In later Jewish law, a minyan of ten is required for communal worship and certain legal acts — this scene is one of the textual anchors for that tradition. Boaz's action at the gate is methodical: he arrived, waited for the nearer kinsman (v1), gathered witnesses, and convened the proceeding before Ruth's potential redemption is named. He controls the legal process to ensure the outcome.
3: vayyomer lagg'el cheleqat hasadeh asher le'achinu le-elimelech machar naomi hashavah misedeh moav: 'He said to the kinsman: “Naomi, who has returned from Moab, is selling the piece of land that belonged to our kinsman Elimelech.”' Boaz introduces the land transaction first, before Ruth is mentioned. This sequencing is deliberate: the nearer kinsman agrees to redeem the land (v4), presumably calculating the financial benefit of adding land to his holdings. Only after the kinsman agrees does Boaz spring the full obligation (v5) — Ruth comes with the land. The legal strategy is careful: Boaz presents the terms in stages to ensure the nearer kinsman's own refusal.
4: va'ani amarti e'elah oznecha lemor qeneh neged hayoshvim: 'I thought I should inform you, saying: Buy it before those sitting here.' Boaz's obligation to inform the nearer kinsman first is the goel protocol in action: the closest kinsman has first right of redemption. Ge'al-lach ata (redeem it yourself) — Boaz gives the nearer kinsman the right of first refusal in proper legal sequence. ve'im lo yig'al haggedah-li ve'edah ki ein zulatecha lig'ol va'anochi acharecha: 'And if you will not redeem it, tell me, so that I may know. For there is no one other than you to redeem it, and I come after you.' Boaz transparently discloses both the priority order and his own position in it — he is not hiding his interest.
8: vayyomer hagg'el leBoaz qeneh-lach vayyishlof na'alo: 'So the kinsman said to Boaz: “Buy it for yourself.” And he drew off his sandal.' The sandal ceremony (shalaf na'alo) formalizes the transfer of redemption rights. Deut 25:9-10 describes a related but different ceremony (the widow pulling off the refusing brother's sandal as an act of shame in the levirate refusal). Here the kinsman himself removes his own sandal and gives it to Boaz — not a shame ceremony but a voluntary transfer of rights. Ruth 4:7 explains this was the custom to 'confirm all matters in Israel.' The symbolic logic: the foot that walked the land is released; Boaz's foot will now walk it.
9: vayyomer boaz lazeqenim vekhol ha'am edim atem hayom ki qaniti et-kol-asher le-elimelech: 'Then Boaz said to the elders and all the people: “You are witnesses today that I have purchased all that belonged to Elimelech.”' Edim atem hayom (you are witnesses today) — the formulaic witness declaration before the assembled gate. Boaz names all the property transferred: Elimelech's land, plus what belonged to Chilion and Mahlon. This is the comprehensive kinsman-redemption (ge'ulah) of the full family estate — land, name, and legacy.
11: vayyomru kol ha'am asher bassha'ar vehazeqenim edim yiten YHWH et-ha'isha haba'ah el-beitcha kerache'l ukhele'ah asher banu sheteihen et beit Yisrael: 'All the people who were at the gate and the elders said: “We are witnesses. May YHWH make the woman who is coming into your house like Rachel and Leah, who together built the house of Israel.”' The communal blessing invokes the matriarchs Rachel and Leah — the Moabite Ruth is blessed to be like the founding mothers of Israel. The boundary between insider and outsider is dissolved by covenant incorporation: a Moabite is now prayed over using the names of Israel's greatest matriarchs. ase chayil be-efratah uqera shem beveit lechem: 'may you act worthily (ase chayil) in Ephrathah and call forth a name (uqera shem) in Bethlehem' — Boaz is exhorted in the same chayil vocabulary used of both him (2:1) and Ruth (3:11).
12: uveithcha yihyeh keveit perets asher yaldah tamar leyehudah min-hazera asher yitten YHWH lecha min-hana'arah hazot: 'And may your house be like the house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah — from the offspring that YHWH will give you from this young woman.' The Perez-Tamar allusion is significant: Tamar (a woman of ambiguous ethnic status who used a provocative encounter to secure her levirate rights from Judah) is Ruth's exact narrative parallel. Both women took bold action to secure their place in the covenant line when the men responsible for them failed. The Perez lineage is already the line from which Boaz descends (v18-22 — the closing genealogy begins with Perez). The witnesses' blessing links the current story backward to its most exact prototype.
13: vayyiqqach boaz et-rut vatehi lo le'isha vayavo eleiha vayitten YHWH lah herayon vatteled ben: 'So Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife. When he went in to her, YHWH granted her conception, and she bore a son.' The birth of the son is YHWH's direct act: vayitten YHWH lah herayon (YHWH granted her conception). Ruth's previous barrenness (she had been married ten years to Mahlon without children, 1:4-5) is resolved by divine gift. The son is not primarily Boaz and Ruth's son but Naomi's restoration: the women of the neighborhood will name him for Naomi (v17). YHWH's providence, worked through human chesed, is complete.
14: vatomerena hanashim el-naomi baruch YHWH asher lo hishbit lach go'el hayom: 'The women said to Naomi: “Blessed be YHWH who has not left you this day without a kinsman-redeemer (go'el).”' The women's doxology names the newborn Obed as a go'el — a kinsman-redeemer. The goel arc has moved: Boaz was the redemptive agent, now Obed is the living fulfillment of what the redemption secured. The goel terminology applied to an infant echoes the messianic trajectory: the redeemer begins as a child. veyiqara shemo beyisrael: 'may his name be called forth in Israel' — anticipating his famous grandson David.
15: vehayah lach lemeshiv nefesh ulekhalkkel et-sevvatecha ki challateach asher ahavattecha yeladathu: 'He will restore your life (lemeshiv nefesh) and sustain you in your old age, for your daughter-in-law who loves you, who is more to you than seven sons, bore him.' Meshiv nefesh (restorer of life/soul) is applied to Obed, but through Ruth. The phrase asher ahavattecha (who loves you) — Ruth's love for Naomi is the theological engine of the entire book. It is the love of Ruth (chesed enacted as ahavah) that drives every plot movement. The extraordinary claim — Ruth is 'better than seven sons' to you — inverts the patriarchal calculus that had made sons the supreme value. Ruth's chesed exceeds the most valued thing in her cultural world.
16: vatikach naomi et-hayeled vatashehu vecheiqah vatehi lo le-omenet: 'Naomi took the child and laid him against her chest (vecheiqah) and became his nurse.' Cheiq (chest/lap/bosom) is the intimate holding space — the same word used for Moses's mother holding him (Exod 4:6) and for the servant of the poor man holding his ewe lamb (2 Sam 12:3). Naomi, who described herself as 'empty' (1:21) and unable to provide sons (1:11-12), holds the child as if he were her own son — because through the goel transaction, he legally preserves Elimelech's name (v10). The empty has been filled; the bitter has become sweet — the book completes the reversal it began with Naomi's self-renaming.
19: vechetzron holid et-ram veram holid et-amminadav: 'Hezron fathered Ram, and Ram fathered Amminadab.' The genealogy descends through Hezron (son of Perez, son of Judah, son of Jacob) — these are the tribal ancestors of the Davidic line. Hezron appears in the genealogy of Num 26:21 and 1 Chr 2:9-10. Ram is the father of Amminadab, whose daughter Elisheba became Aaron's wife (Exod 6:23) — a point linking this genealogy to the priestly line as well as the royal line. The ten-generation list (vv18-22) spanning Perez to David maps the chosen lineage through the Bethlehem clan.
20: ve'amminadav holid et-nachshon venachshon holid et-salmah: 'Amminadab fathered Nahshon, and Nahshon fathered Salmon.' Nachshon (Nahshon son of Amminadab) was the chieftain of the tribe of Judah in the wilderness (Num 1:7; 2:3; 7:12) and the first to cross the Red Sea according to one tradition. His prominence in the wilderness generation connects the Ruth genealogy to the Exodus era. Salmah/Salmon is likely the father of Boaz who took Rahab as wife (see Matt 1:5 — 'Salmon the father of Boaz by Rahab'), another foreign woman in the Davidic lineage. The genealogy preserves the memory of foreign-woman inclusion: Tamar, Rahab, Ruth — all non-Israelites incorporated into the line that leads to David and ultimately to Christ.
21: vesalmah holid et-boaz uvoaz holid et-oved: 'Salmon fathered Boaz, and Boaz fathered Obed.' The names now carry the full weight of the narrative. Boaz — whom we have known for the entire book as the gibbor chayil, the redeemer, the one who spoke blessing and performed chesed — appears here as a link in a chain: not merely a character but a hinge in the genealogy of salvation. Obed — whose name means 'servant/worshiper' — is the son born from the goel transaction, the one who fills Naomi's empty arms and preserves Elimelech's name.
22: ve'oved holid et-yishai veyishai holid et-david: 'Obed fathered Jesse, and Jesse fathered David.' The final verse of Ruth is the book's destination: David. The entire narrative — the famine, the Moabite marriage, the three deaths, Naomi's emptiness, Ruth's loyalty, Boaz's redemption, Obed's birth — has been moving toward this name. Ruth's Christological significance begins here: Matt 1:5 includes her in the genealogy of Jesus ('Boaz the father of Obed by Ruth, and Obed the father of Jesse, and Jesse the father of David the king'). The Moabite woman who chose chesed and YHWH's people over her own becomes the great-grandmother of Israel's greatest king and an ancestor of the Messiah. The boundary-crossing she enacted in ch1 — leaving her gods, her people, her homeland — is the same boundary-crossing that the gospel enacts: bringing those far off into the covenant community.
